thread.title: Democratic attorneys general brace for legal battles with Trump’s return
thread.title_full: Democratic attorneys general brace for legal battles with Trump’s return

author: Sam Brodey
title: Democratic attorneys general brace for legal battles with Trump’s return
text:
WASHINGTON — Over two years ago, well before President-elect Donald Trump launched his comeback bid , government attorneys in the California capital of Sacramento were busy preparing for his potential return.
In the summer of 2022, after the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade , Attorney General Rob Bonta directed his staff to write a brief challenging a hypothetical national abortion ban.
The idea, Bonta said, was to “go through the exercise of examining potential arguments and their merit” well ahead of time, should Trump move to enact an abortion ban if reelected. They ultimately ran the same exercise with other potential Trump policies, from immigration enforcement to environmental regulation .
“We’ve been preparing for months, in some cases years, for the possibility of a Trump administration 2.0,” Bonta said.
Advertisement
For Democrats, that work will urgently, and uneasily, come due in January, once Trump takes the oath of office again. Bonta is far from alone in his preparation for that moment: many of the 25 Democratic state attorneys general who will be in office next year have poured time and resources into getting ready for a second Trump term.
Get Starting Point A guide through the most important stories of the morning, delivered Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Enter Email Sign Up Related : Courts blocked a lot in Trump’s first term. His opponents have already been prepping for round two. Several attorneys general were reluctant to discuss specific plans, saying they wanted to see what Trump’s administration would do first. But they’ve studied up on his previous actions and consulted with attorneys general who served during his last term.
The day after the election, Vermont Attorney General Charity Clark recalled, “I said, ‘Today we grieve, tomorrow we plan.’ Except for me, because I was already planning.”
So was Massachusetts Attorney General Andrea Campbell, who said she and a group of Democratic attorneys general convened before the election to work on “contingency planning.”
“My eyes are wide open that this is a president-elect, along with some folks he has nominated to serve . . . that [doesn’t] respect the rule of law,” Campbell said. “They are out there demonizing immigrants and those of the LGBTQ+ community, and we have an obligation to defend our constituents.”
Related : Here are the people Donald Trump has picked for key positions so far With Democrats swept out of power in Washington, state-level Democrats in power are set to take the lead in opposing Trump’s agenda. State attorneys general are the party’s first — and perhaps best — check on Trump policies they believe could run afoul of the law.
Advertisement
Democratic attorneys general played the same role during the first Trump administration, filing hundreds of lawsuits challenging actions like his attempt to block travelers from Muslim-majority countries from entering the United States, which was stalled but ultimately upheld by the Supreme Court.
Collectively, they earned a high success rate, winning over 83 percent of their cases against the Trump administration, according to Paul Nolette, a political science professor at Marquette University who tracks attorneys general legal activity.
New Jersey Attorney General Matthew Platkin said, “We are better prepared than we were at this point in 2016.”
“We were successful in the first term because he violated the law so frequently,” he said. “If he does that again, I predict we’ll be successful again.”
Still, Nolette predicted the overall environment may be worse for Democrats than it was during the first Trump administration. Trump’s allies have also been planning and are ready to move aggressively on drastic measures — such as chipping away at birthright citizenship, which is reportedly under consideration.
Administration staff are expected to be more aligned with Trump this time , and the federal courts could be a more favorable audience, too. Trump appointed more than 200 judges, and the Supreme Court is now firmly in the control of its conservative majority . “The agencies are going to be more linked to Trump’s agenda and more prepared to defend [their policies] in court,” Nolette said.
Many successful attorney general challenges during the first term centered on violations of the Administrative Procedures Act, which governs the process for how federal agencies write, and change, regulations. Judges often found that the first Trump administration’s eagerness to rapidly undo a variety of rules set by the Obama administration led to violations.
Related : Trump, after distancing himself from Project 2025, picks people who worked on it Advertisement
Asked about the prospect of challenges from Democratic attorneys general, Senator Mike Lee, a Utah Republican and Senate Judiciary Committee member, said he expects more challenges in the Administrative Procedures Act vein. “As to what else they might bring, it’s hard to say,” said Lee.
In response to an inquiry, Trump-Vance transition spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said, “The American people reelected President Trump by a resounding margin giving him a mandate to implement the promises he made on the campaign trail. He will deliver.”
Indeed, many Democratic officials are mindful of the message sent by Trump’s performance — a popular vote victory as well as a sweep of the key battleground states — and wary of the perception they are working to stop his agenda before he even takes office.
“I’m not waking up every day dying to sue the president of the United States,” Platkin, the New Jersey attorney general, said. “He’s well within his rights to implement his policy agenda as he sees fit for the country. What he is not within his rights to do is violate the laws and the Constitution of the United States.”
Across the board, however, attorneys general expect Trump will offer up plenty of opportunities for court challenges.
Allies of the incoming administration have been unusually specific in outlining what it might pursue. Each attorney general who spoke to the Globe mentioned Project 2025, the 900-page blueprint for a second Trump term outlined by the Heritage Foundation think tank , as a study guide of sorts.
Advertisement
The document, written by Trump loyalists but disavowed by Trump on the campaign trail , prepared Democrats for a specific but potentially massive fight over reproductive rights. Project 2025 explains how an administration could use an obscure 19th-century law called the Comstock Act, which governs what can be sent via mail, to ban medications that induce abortion.
Related : Could Donald Trump really deport undocumented immigrants who live in Mass.? Clark, whose state of Vermont staunchly supports abortion rights, said she is watching the issue closely. “Whatever tricks they’re going to try to pull, I will be there,” she said, something several other attorneys general echoed.
Trump and his top allies have also talked extensively about their plan to launch a program of mass deportation of undocumented immigrants , which also came up frequently as a potential target for litigation from Democratic attorneys general.
A number of challenges in other policy realms likely await if Trump makes good on promises like eliminating the federal Department of Education or undoing federal gun safety rules , said Platkin.
Campbell said she expects addressing other issues that could be at the forefront of fights with the federal government — climate policy, affordability, gun violence prevention — will be a “continuation of the work” the state was doing prior to Trump’s election.
Related : Trump pledged to close the Education Department. What would that mean? Changes in the federal administration, Campbell predicted, may result in her office’s work being “a little bit more reactive than it was before.” But, she added, “we’re not going to let this new administration consume everything we’re doing.”
Further complicating their jobs is a lingering question about who will head up the federal Justice Department as attorney general, which oversees the administration’s broad response to state legal challenges. Trump’s first pick, former representative Matt Gaetz, withdrew his nomination Thursday amid scrutiny of allegations he paid for sex with underage girls .
Advertisement
